extra.reasoning: The property G(opted_out implies not sent) holds. The only transition that sets sent:=true is t2 (send_attempt), whose guard requires not opted_out, so sent can only become true while opted_out is false. The only transition that sets opted_out:=true is t1 (optout), which atomically also sets sent:=false in the same update. Therefore there is no way to enter or remain in a state where opted_out is true with sent true. Inductive proof of the invariant I = (opted_out implies not sent): it holds in the initial state (opted_out=false). For each transition from a state satisfying I: t1 yields opted_out=true, sent=false (I holds); t2 fires only when opted_out=false and leaves opted_out=false (antecedent false, I holds); t3 leaves opted_out unchanged and sets sent=false (I holds); t4 leaves opted_out unchanged and sets sent=false (I holds). The bad state (opted_out=true, sent=true) is unreachable, so the property holds for all executions (the 7-event bound and beyond).
